era_context: The Thirty Years' War continues with Swedish intervention under Gustav II Adolf; the Qing Dynasty (Later Jin) consolidates power in Manchuria; the Ottoman Empire faces internal challenges; European colonial expansion accelerates in North America and the Caribbean; the Scientific Revolution gains momentum in Europe.

events.title: Death of Gustav II Adolf at the Battle of Lützen
events.description: King Gustav II Adolf of Sweden, the dominant military figure of the Thirty Years' War, was killed in the Battle of Lützen on 6 November 1633. Though the Swedish forces achieved a tactical victory, Gustav's death marked a turning point. He was succeeded by his daughter Christina (then 8 years old) under a regency. His death weakened Swedish military dominance and shifted the balance of power in the war.

sources.name: Contemporary Swedish and Imperial military records; Pufendorf's Historia Belli Suecici
Multiple eyewitness accounts from both sides

events.certainty_note: The date, location, and circumstances of Gustav's death are well-documented in multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: Galileo's trial and house arrest
events.description: Galileo Galilei was tried by the Roman Inquisition for his support of heliocentrism, particularly his 1632 publication of the Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems. On 22 June 1633, he was found guilty of heresy and sentenced to house arrest in Arcetri near Florence, where he remained until his death in 1642. This case became emblematic of the conflict between scientific inquiry and religious authority.

sources.name: Inquisition trial records and papal documents

events.certainty_note: The trial date and verdict are precisely documented in Inquisition records.

events.title: Death of John Oldham (English trader)
events.description: English trader John Oldham was killed by Pequot or Narragansett warriors in July 1633 (some sources date this to 1634). His death was one of the precipitating incidents for English military action against the Pequot Nation and contributed to escalating tensions that would lead to the Pequot War (1636–1638).
events.certainty_note: The event is documented in colonial records, but dating varies between 1633 and 1634 in different sources. The perpetrators are not definitively identified.

events.title: Qing Dynasty: Hong Taiji declares himself emperor
events.description: Hong Taiji, leader of the Later Jin state, declared himself emperor and renamed the state 'Qing' (清), establishing the Qing Dynasty in 1636 (though some sources date the declaration to 1633). This formalized the transition from a Jurchen tribal confederation to a centralized empire modeled on Chinese imperial traditions. Hong Taiji died in 1643, but his reforms laid the groundwork for the Qing conquest of Ming China.

sources.name: Chinese chronicles and later Qing histories

events.certainty_note: The declaration of the Qing Dynasty is dated variously to 1633 or 1636 depending on the source. The 1636 date is more commonly used in modern scholarship.

events.title: Wallenstein's dismissal from Imperial command
events.description: Albrecht von Wallenstein, the powerful Imperial general, was dismissed from command by Emperor Ferdinand II in August 1633, partly due to his independent military operations and partly due to court intrigue. Wallenstein had become too powerful and autonomous for the Emperor's comfort. His dismissal weakened Imperial military effectiveness and contributed to the shifting balance of the Thirty Years' War.
events.certainty_note: The dismissal date and circumstances are well-documented in Imperial records and Wallenstein's correspondence.

events.title: Tokugawa shogunate: Sakoku edicts intensify
events.description: The Tokugawa shogunate under Tokugawa Iemitsu issued increasingly strict edicts restricting foreign contact and Christian missionary activity. These edicts were part of the sakoku (closed country) policy that would isolate Japan from most foreign trade and influence for over two centuries. The policy reflected concerns about Christian conversion and foreign destabilization.
key_figures: Tokugawa Ieyasu (founder, still influential through legacy)
events.certainty_note: The sakoku edicts are well-documented in Tokugawa records and contemporary foreign accounts.

events.title: Ottoman-Safavid War: Siege of Baghdad
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murad IV besieged and captured Baghdad from Safavid control in 1638 (but the campaign began in 1633). This was part of the ongoing Ottoman-Safavid conflict over control of Mesopotamia and the Caucasus. The capture of Baghdad represented a significant Ottoman military success and shifted regional power dynamics.

sources.name: Ottoman imperial records and military chronicles
Safavid chronicles and later Ottoman histories

events.certainty_note: The campaign dates are documented, but the exact chronology of the siege varies in different sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Hong Taiji's declaration as Qing emperor is disputed; some sources place it in 1633, others in 1636. Modern scholarship generally favors 1636. The perpetrators of John Oldham's death are not definitively identified in colonial records, though Pequot involvement is often assumed. Wallenstein's dismissal was more complex than simple court intrigue; military and financial disputes also played roles.
historiographic_note: 1633 is exceptionally well-documented for European events, particularly the Thirty Years' War and the Galileo trial, with abundant primary sources. Asian sources (Qing, Japanese, Ottoman) are also substantial but reflect official court perspectives. The Galileo trial is particularly well-preserved due to Inquisition record-keeping. Colonial North American sources are limited and heavily biased toward English perspectives; indigenous viewpoints are largely absent. Ottoman-Safavid conflict sources are primarily from Ottoman records, with Safavid perspectives underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Gustav's death weakened Swedish position, contributing to the Imperial-Spanish victory at Nördlingen
Galileo's house arrest lasted until his death in 1642
Wallenstein's dismissal weakened Imperial military coordination before Nördlingen

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
